front.title: The ATTR_DISCARD Path Attribute for BGP UPDATE Messages

abstract.t: RFC 7606 defines revised error handling for BGP UPDATE messages, including an "attribute discard" action where a malformed optional attribute is removed and the UPDATE continues to be processed. Complying with this requirement means removing the attribute by reconstructing the path attributes section.

This document defines the ATTR_DISCARD path attribute, an optional attribute that serves as an in-place marker. When a BGP speaker applies attribute-discard per RFC 7606, it overwrites the malformed attribute's flags, type code, and first two value bytes, preserving the wire buffer layout without data movement. The value contains one or more 2-byte (code, reason) pairs identifying each discarded attribute and the reason for its removal; when multiple attributes are discarded from the same UPDATE, a single ATTR_DISCARD carries all pairs. The Transitive bit is inherited from the original attribute, allowing implementations to choose whether the marker propagates to peers or remains local.
ATTR_DISCARD is a wire-level construct. It is not stored in the Adj-RIB-In and does not affect route selection.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: BGP uses UPDATE messages to convey routing information between peers. Each UPDATE message may contain a set of path attributes that describe properties of the advertised routes.
RFC 7606 Section 2 defines graduated error-handling actions for UPDATE messages. The mildest, "attribute discard," states:
section.blockquote: "The attribute MUST be discarded, and the UPDATE message continues to be processed."
section.t: This document defines an alternative mechanism that satisfies the MUST without requiring data movement, while also informing downstream peers that an attribute was discarded: overwrite the malformed attribute's header and first two value bytes in place with a standardized marker, the ATTR_DISCARD path attribute. The original attribute ceases to exist in the wire representation.
ATTR_DISCARD inherits the Transitive bit from the original attribute. This produces two behaviors:

ul.li: If the original attribute was transitive (e.g., COMMUNITY, AGGREGATOR), the marker propagates along the AS path.
If the original attribute was non-transitive (e.g., ORIGINATOR_ID, CLUSTER_LIST), the marker remains local.

section.t: Implementations MAY clear the Transitive bit at generation time, ensuring the marker never propagates regardless of the original attribute's transitivity. Implementations MUST NOT set the Transitive bit if it was not set in the original attribute.
This document uses "attribute discard" (lowercase) to refer to the error-handling action defined in RFC 7606, and "ATTR_DISCARD" (uppercase) to refer to the path attribute defined herein.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Problem Statement
section.t: Consider a BGP speaker that receives an UPDATE with the following path attributes:

Per RFC 7606 Section 7.7, a malformed AGGREGATOR (wrong length for the negotiated AS size) calls for attribute-discard. The implementation must "discard" the AGGREGATOR attribute while preserving the UPDATE for continued processing.
Removing the AGGREGATOR requires rebuilding the path attributes section (see ).

The ATTR_DISCARD attribute solves this by overwriting the attribute in place: the flags byte, the type code byte, the first two value bytes (which record the original type code and a reason code), and the remaining value bytes (which are zeroed). No bytes are shifted. The zeroed bytes ensure that no remnant of the malformed attribute's data persists in the wire buffer. (The zero-copy benefit applies primarily to the receive path and intra-AS forwarding; see for trade-offs on the outbound path under non-default forwarding policies.)
After applying ATTR_DISCARD to the malformed AGGREGATOR:

The formula produces 0x80 | (0xC0 & 0x50) = 0xC0, which happens to match the original flags because AGGREGATOR is already optional and transitive. The type code is overwritten with TBD. The first value byte contains 0x07 (the original AGGREGATOR type code); the second value byte contains 0x02 (reason: invalid length). The marker inherits the Transitive bit, so downstream speakers will forward it with Partial bit set per RFC 4271 Section 5.

dl.dd: Variable. When generated by in-place overwrite, inherited from the overwritten attribute. When generated by rebuild (), equal to 2 * N where N is the number of discarded attributes.

A sequence of 2-byte (code, reason) pairs. Each pair's first byte is the original attribute type code; each pair's second byte is the reason code (see ). When generated by in-place overwrite, trailing bytes beyond the first pair are zeroed.

section.name: Transitivity Inheritance
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD is always Optional, regardless of whether the original attribute was well-known or optional. The Optional bit must be set so that a non-recognizing speaker can process the attribute safely: a well-known attribute code that is not recognized triggers a NOTIFICATION (, Section 5), which would tear down the session. Unlike most BGP path attributes, ATTR_DISCARD does not have a fixed Transitive bit. Instead, the Transitive bit is inherited from the original attribute that was overwritten.
When the original attribute was transitive, the marker is forwarded per RFC 4271 Section 5. When non-transitive, the marker is silently ignored by non-recognizing speakers.
The flag requirements for ATTR_DISCARD are:

dl.dd: MUST match the length field encoding (set if length > 255)

section.t: The general formula for computing the overwritten flags byte:
new_flags = 0x80 | (original_flags & 0x50)
Where:

ul.li: 0x80 sets the Optional bit
0x50 is the mask for Transitive (0x40) and Extended Length (0x10) bits; Partial (0x20) is excluded so that the Partial bit is cleared (see below)

section.t: The Partial bit is cleared at generation. When a downstream speaker forwards an unrecognized optional transitive attribute, it sets the Partial bit per RFC 4271 Section 5. This allows receivers to distinguish between "generated by the discarding speaker" (Partial=0) and "forwarded through intermediate speakers" (Partial=1).

section.name: Wire Format
section.t: The ATTR_DISCARD value consists of zero or more 2-byte pairs. Each pair contains one type-code octet (the original attribute type code) followed by one reason-code octet (). Trailing octets from in-place overwrite are zeroed.

When a single attribute is discarded and its value length is at least 2, the implementation MAY overwrite the attribute in place: modify the flags byte (applying the formula from ), overwrite the type code with TBD, write the (code, reason) pair into the first two value bytes, and zero the remainder. The length field is not modified; consequently the value length is inherited from the original attribute and may not be a multiple of 2. Trailing bytes beyond the first pair are zeroed.

When multiple attributes are discarded, or when the original attribute has fewer than 2 value bytes, the implementation MUST remove the affected attributes by rebuilding the path attributes section and insert a single ATTR_DISCARD attribute whose value contains one (code, reason) pair per discarded attribute. The value length is exactly 2 * N where N is the number of pairs (see and ).
Example 1: Transitive original (AGGREGATOR, flags 0xC0)
Before overwrite:
After overwrite:
Flags unchanged (AGGREGATOR is already optional transitive). Reason 0x02: invalid length.
Example 2: Non-transitive original (ORIGINATOR_ID, flags 0x80)
Before overwrite:
After overwrite:

Flags unchanged (optional non-transitive). Reason 0x01: EBGP invalid. ORIGINATOR_ID is used by route reflectors on IBGP sessions; its presence on an EBGP session is invalid per RFC 7606 Section 7.9. The marker remains local (non-transitive).
Example 3: Well-known transitive original (LOCAL_PREF from EBGP, flags 0x40)
Before overwrite:
After overwrite:
Flags change from 0x40 to 0xC0 (Optional bit set). Reason 0x01: EBGP invalid. LOCAL_PREF from an EBGP peer is a discard per RFC 7606 Section 7.5.
Example 4: Extended length non-transitive attribute (AIGP, flags 0x90)
Before overwrite:
After overwrite:

Formula: 0x80 | (0x90 & 0x50) = 0x80 | 0x10 = 0x90. Flags unchanged because AIGP is already optional non-transitive. Extended Length bit preserved; 2-byte length field is unchanged. Original type code 0x1A (AIGP). Reason 0x03: malformed value. The marker is non-transitive and will not propagate.

section.name: Reason Codes
section.t: The following reason codes are defined:

tr.td: Reason not recorded or not applicable

Attribute discarded because it was received from an external peer and is not valid in EBGP context (e.g., LOCAL_PREF per RFC 7606 Section 7.5, ORIGINATOR_ID per RFC 7606 Section 7.9, CLUSTER_LIST per RFC 7606 Section 7.10)

Attribute length does not match the expected length for the attribute type (e.g., AGGREGATOR with wrong length per RFC 7606 Section 7.7, ATOMIC_AGGREGATE with non-zero length per Section 7.6, LARGE COMMUNITY with length not a multiple of 12 per )
Attribute value is syntactically invalid despite a correct length (e.g., unrecognized AIGP TLV type per Section 3)
Attribute deliberately removed by local policy (e.g., filtering an unknown optional transitive attribute that the operator does not wish to propagate)
Available for future allocation by IANA

section.t: Implementations MUST set the reason code to the most specific applicable value. If no specific code applies, implementations MUST use 0 (Unspecified).

Receivers that encounter an unrecognized reason code MUST treat it as Unspecified for logging and counter purposes; the unrecognized value MUST NOT cause the ATTR_DISCARD marker to be treated as malformed.

section.name: Generating ATTR_DISCARD (Sender)
section.t: If the UPDATE is subject to a more severe error-handling action than attribute discard (e.g., treat-as-withdraw per RFC 7606 Section 3.h), the ATTR_DISCARD mechanism MUST NOT be applied. The more severe action subsumes the attribute discard.
When one or more attributes require discarding, the speaker first determines which procedure to use:

ul.li: If exactly one attribute requires discarding and its value length is at least 2: the in-place overwrite procedure below MAY be used.
Otherwise (multiple attributes, or value length less than 2): the rebuild procedure in MUST be used.

section.t: The in-place overwrite procedure for a single attribute:

ol.li: Locate the malformed attribute's header in the path attributes byte sequence. The header starts at the flags byte.

li.t: This sets the Optional bit, preserves the Transitive bit and Extended Length bit, and clears the Partial bit.

ol.li: Save the original type code byte (for use in step 5 or local logging).
Overwrite the type code byte with the ATTR_DISCARD code (TBD).
Overwrite the first byte of the attribute value with the original type code saved in step 3. (The precondition for the in-place procedure guarantees value length >= 2.)
Overwrite the second value byte with the reason code (): 1 for EBGP invalid, 2 for invalid length, 3 for malformed value, 4 for local policy, or 0 if no specific reason applies.
Zero all remaining value bytes (bytes 2 through N-1, where N is the attribute value length). The length field MUST NOT be modified.
Continue processing the UPDATE message normally per RFC 7606.

section.t: The following table shows the minimum value length when each attribute-discard trigger fires:

table.name: Attribute-Discard Triggers and Minimum Value Lengths

section.t: Certain attribute-discard triggers are error-based (reason codes 2 and 3) while others are contextual (reason code 1). Implementations MAY choose to use conventional removal for contextual discards and reserve ATTR_DISCARD for genuine malformation cases.

An implementation MAY also use the ATTR_DISCARD mechanism to remove unknown optional transitive attributes that local policy does not wish to propagate. In this case, the original attribute is overwritten using the same procedure () with reason code 4 (Local Policy). This informs downstream speakers that an attribute was deliberately filtered rather than silently dropped.
An implementation MAY override the inherited Transitive bit at generation time by clearing it:
new_flags = 0x80 | (original_flags & 0x10)

This produces a non-transitive marker regardless of the original attribute's transitivity. This is useful when the operator does not want to propagate error-handling artifacts to peers. Conversely, an implementation MUST NOT set the Transitive bit if it was not set in the original attribute; promoting a non-transitive attribute to transitive scope at generation time would violate the original attribute's propagation intent. (Note: the "propagate" forwarding policy in may set the Transitive bit at forwarding time on a received marker; this restriction applies only to the initial generation of the ATTR_DISCARD overwrite.)
The choice between inheriting and clearing the Transitive bit SHOULD be configurable, with the default being to inherit.

A BGP speaker MUST NOT generate ATTR_DISCARD except when applying the "attribute discard" action per RFC 7606 or when local policy removes an attribute from a received UPDATE (see reason code 4 in ). In particular, ATTR_DISCARD MUST NOT appear in a freshly originated UPDATE.

When a well-known attribute is discarded (e.g., LOCAL_PREF received from an EBGP peer per RFC 7606 Section 7.5), the implementation MUST ensure that any attributes required by the destination session type are present before forwarding. For example, LOCAL_PREF MUST be added with an appropriate default value when forwarding to IBGP peers, per Section 5.1.1. This requirement applies regardless of whether ATTR_DISCARD or conventional removal was used.
A BGP speaker SHOULD log the original attribute code and the reason for discard when generating an ATTR_DISCARD marker.

If multiple attributes independently require the "attribute discard" action, the speaker MUST remove all of them and insert a single ATTR_DISCARD whose value contains one (code, reason) pair per discarded attribute. See .

ATTR_DISCARD has no attribute ordering requirement. When generated by in-place overwrite, it occupies the position of the original attribute, which may result in path attributes not appearing in ascending type code order. Implementations MUST NOT reject an UPDATE solely because an ATTR_DISCARD attribute appears out of type code order. When generated by rebuild, ATTR_DISCARD MAY be placed at any position in the path attributes section.

Section 5 prohibits multiple instances of the same attribute type code in a single UPDATE. If the received UPDATE already contains an ATTR_DISCARD attribute from an upstream speaker, and the local speaker needs to discard additional attributes, the local speaker MUST use the rebuild procedure: remove the upstream ATTR_DISCARD and all locally- discarded attributes, then insert a single ATTR_DISCARD whose value contains all (code, reason) pairs -- upstream pairs followed by local pairs. The transitivity of the merged ATTR_DISCARD follows the rules in ; in particular, the upstream ATTR_DISCARD's Transitive bit and the local originals' Transitive bits must ALL agree for the result to be transitive.

Merging upstream and local pairs into a single ATTR_DISCARD loses the Partial bit provenance signal (which pairs originated locally vs. upstream). Implementations SHOULD log the upstream pairs separately before merging to preserve diagnostic traceability.

section.name: Receiving ATTR_DISCARD
section.t: The behavior of a receiving BGP speaker depends on whether it recognizes the ATTR_DISCARD attribute code.

section.name: Non-Recognizing Speakers
section.t: Standard RFC 4271 Section 5 processing applies: transitive markers are forwarded with Partial set; non-transitive markers are silently ignored. No implementation changes are required.

section.name: Recognizing Speakers
section.t: A BGP speaker that recognizes the ATTR_DISCARD attribute code:

ul.li: SHOULD parse the value as a sequence of 2-byte (code, reason) pairs. Each pair identifies one discarded attribute and the reason for its removal. The number of pairs is value_length / 2. Any trailing byte (odd value length) is ignored.
SHOULD maintain per-peer counters (see ) and log each (code, reason) pair.
For forwarding behavior, see .

section.name: Malformed ATTR_DISCARD
section.t: Per the guidance of RFC 7606 Section 8, this section defines what constitutes a malformed ATTR_DISCARD attribute and how it is handled.

ATTR_DISCARD has no currently defined malformation conditions. Any value length (including zero) is valid. A value length of zero or one may indicate a non-conforming implementation or a future extension; there are no complete (code, reason) pairs to parse. An odd value length indicates a trailing byte that receivers ignore. Receivers MUST NOT treat any ATTR_DISCARD as malformed based solely on its length.

ATTR_DISCARD has no effect on route selection or installation (). Therefore, per RFC 7606 Section 2, should any future revision define malformation conditions for ATTR_DISCARD, such a malformed marker SHALL be handled using the approach of "attribute discard" (i.e., the marker itself is discarded and the UPDATE continues to be processed).

If an implementation applies the ATTR_DISCARD overwrite mechanism to discard a received ATTR_DISCARD marker, the first (code, reason) pair of the resulting marker will contain the ATTR_DISCARD type code (TBD) rather than the original attribute's type code. The original diagnostic information is lost from the wire. Implementations SHOULD log the received (code, reason) pairs before overwriting.

section.name: Forwarding Policy
section.t: When a BGP speaker that recognizes ATTR_DISCARD receives an UPDATE containing one or more ATTR_DISCARD attributes, the default forwarding behavior follows the Transitive bit as with any optional attribute:

ul.li: Transitive ATTR_DISCARD: forwarded to peers with Partial bit set (RFC 4271 Section 5).
Non-transitive ATTR_DISCARD: not forwarded.

section.t: Implementations SHOULD provide a configurable policy to override this default, with at least the following options:

li.t: Transitive ATTR_DISCARD markers are forwarded within the AS via IBGP (including by non-recognizing route reflectors, which forward unrecognized optional transitive attributes per standard BGP processing).

At the originating AS's EBGP boundary, the sending speaker controls propagation. Under the "inherit" policy, a recognizing EBGP speaker MUST clear the Transitive bit before forwarding the marker to the EBGP peer. This prevents the peer from propagating the marker further. However, because the marker becomes non-transitive, a non-recognizing peer will silently ignore it per RFC 4271 Section 5 -- it will not be visible at the BGP layer on that peer. The marker remains observable via BMP or packet-level inspection at the receiving AS. A recognizing peer will process it normally ().

Non-recognizing EBGP speakers in the originating AS will forward the marker as any unrecognized optional transitive attribute, without containment. Operators who cannot ensure that all their EBGP speakers recognize ATTR_DISCARD SHOULD clear the Transitive bit at generation time () to prevent uncontrolled propagation. This trades intra-AS visibility via non-recognizing route reflectors for reliable containment at the AS boundary.

A recognizing speaker that receives a transitive ATTR_DISCARD from an EBGP peer SHOULD also clear the Transitive bit after processing, as defense in depth. However, the originating AS MUST NOT rely on the receiver's behavior for containment, as the receiver may not support this specification.

Non-transitive ATTR_DISCARD markers are silently ignored by non-recognizing speakers per RFC 4271 Section 5 and are not forwarded by recognizing speakers under the default "inherit" policy. (The "propagate" policy overrides this; see .)

Remove ALL ATTR_DISCARD markers before forwarding, regardless of transitivity. This prevents marker propagation beyond the local speaker, useful when operators do not want to expose error-handling artifacts to peers.

When "strip" is active, the implementation MUST prevent ATTR_DISCARD markers from propagating further. This MAY be achieved by removing ATTR_DISCARD attributes from the forwarded path attributes (requires rebuild), or by clearing the Transitive bit (converting to non-transitive so that non-recognizing speakers silently ignore it per RFC 4271 Section 5). Note that clearing the Transitive bit does not remove the marker from the wire; a recognizing peer will still see it. If complete removal is required, the implementation MUST rebuild the path attributes.

Forward ALL ATTR_DISCARD markers as transitive, overriding the default containment behavior of "inherit" and setting the Transitive bit to 1 if necessary. All markers propagate across AS boundaries, including those for originally non-transitive attributes.

When "propagate" is active and the ATTR_DISCARD marker has the Transitive bit clear, the implementation MUST set the Transitive bit before forwarding. A recognizing speaker that explicitly forwards the attribute MUST clear the Partial bit (setting it to 0), even if it was set by an intermediate non-recognizing speaker, because the forwarding speaker recognizes the attribute (RFC 4271 Section 5 requires Partial only for unrecognized attributes). Non-recognizing speakers that subsequently forward the marker will set the Partial bit per standard processing.

Note: When the "propagate" policy promotes a non-transitive marker to transitive, the resulting marker has Partial=0 (not set by the recognizing forwarder) and Transitive=1. The flags alone do not indicate whether the marker was originally transitive or promoted by policy. However, a receiver that knows the transitivity of the original attribute type code (recorded in the value) can infer that promotion occurred. For example, a transitive ATTR_DISCARD carrying (ORIGINATOR_ID, reason) reveals that a non-transitive attribute was promoted.

section.t: The policy SHOULD be configurable per peer-group or per neighbor, allowing operators to strip markers on external sessions while propagating them within their own network.

section.name: Interaction with Route Reflectors
section.t: Route reflectors reflect ATTR_DISCARD markers according to the Transitive bit and the configured forwarding policy ().

section.name: Interaction with Confederation Boundaries
section.t: At confederation boundaries , ATTR_DISCARD markers are handled according to their Transitive bit, per standard RFC 5065 processing. The forwarding policy () MAY be applied.

section.name: Interaction with ATTR_SET
section.t: RFC 6368 defines the ATTR_SET attribute, which contains an embedded set of path attributes. If a malformed attribute is found inside an ATTR_SET value, the ATTR_DISCARD mechanism defined in this document MUST NOT be applied to the embedded attribute.

ATTR_DISCARD operates on top-level path attributes only. It relies on overwriting the attribute header at a known offset in the path attributes byte sequence; embedded attributes within ATTR_SET have a different encapsulation and may be subject to additional validation by the ATTR_SET consumer.

Malformed attributes inside ATTR_SET SHOULD be handled per the error handling rules of RFC 6368 and RFC 7606. If the ATTR_SET itself is malformed (e.g., its contained attributes cannot be parsed), the error handling action for ATTR_SET as a whole applies.

section.name: Multiple Discards in the Same UPDATE
section.t: When multiple attributes in the same UPDATE independently require the "attribute discard" action, the speaker MUST remove all affected attributes by rebuilding the path attributes section and insert a single ATTR_DISCARD attribute whose value contains one (code, reason) pair per discarded attribute. The value length is 2 * N where N is the number of discarded attributes. If the value length exceeds 255, the Extended Length bit (0x10) MUST be set and the length field MUST use two bytes.

If all discarded attributes were transitive, the flags MUST be set to 0xC0 (optional transitive). If all were non-transitive, the flags MUST be set to 0x80 (optional non-transitive). If the discarded attributes have mixed transitivity, the flags SHOULD be set to 0x80 (optional non-transitive) as the conservative default; the "propagate" forwarding policy () can override this at forwarding time if broader visibility is desired. In all cases, the Extended Length bit (0x10) MUST be OR'd into the flags if the value length exceeds 255.

When merging with an upstream ATTR_DISCARD (), "all discarded attributes" includes both the upstream speaker's original discards -- whose aggregate transitivity is represented by the upstream ATTR_DISCARD's Transitive bit -- and the locally discarded attributes. The merged ATTR_DISCARD is transitive (0xC0) only if the upstream ATTR_DISCARD was transitive AND all locally discarded attributes were also transitive. Any disagreement between upstream and local transitivity constitutes mixed transitivity and the conservative default (0x80) applies.

section.name: Diagnostic Visibility
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD attributes in received UPDATEs carry the following information:

ul.li: An upstream speaker applied the attribute-discard action per RFC 7606.
The Partial bit distinguishes locally-generated markers (Partial=0) from forwarded markers (Partial=1).
For in-place overwrites, the inherited length field indicates the size of the original attribute.
Transitive markers are visible at any point along the AS path.

section.t: Implementations SHOULD make ATTR_DISCARD attributes visible in diagnostic outputs (route dumps, packet traces, monitoring systems) with clear labeling, for example:

section.name: Counting Discarded Attributes
section.t: Implementations SHOULD maintain the following counters:

ul.li: attr-discard-applied: Number of attributes overwritten with ATTR_DISCARD on received UPDATEs (per peer, per original attribute code, per reason code).

attr-discard-received: Number of (code, reason) pairs observed in received ATTR_DISCARD attributes (per peer, per original attribute code, per reason code). A non-zero value indicates that an upstream speaker is performing RFC 7606 attribute-discard with this mechanism.

attr-discard-received-partial: Subset of attr-discard-received where the Partial bit is set, indicating the marker was forwarded through at least one intermediate speaker (the discard did not occur at the immediate peer).

section.t: Note: Per , a single ATTR_DISCARD marker carries one (code, reason) pair per discarded attribute. The attr-discard-received counter SHOULD be incremented once per pair. The attr-discard-applied counter tracks all local attribute-discard actions per RFC 7606, regardless of whether the ATTR_DISCARD wire mechanism or conventional removal was used.

section.name: Backward Compatibility
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD requires no capability negotiation. Non-recognizing speakers apply standard RFC 4271 Section 5 processing to the attribute based on its flags. The wire format of the UPDATE is unchanged: the total path attributes length and all attribute boundaries are preserved.

In mixed deployments, the originating AS controls propagation via the forwarding policy (). When not all EBGP speakers in the AS recognize ATTR_DISCARD, the generating speaker SHOULD clear the Transitive bit at generation time () to prevent uncontrolled propagation.

section.name: RIB Storage
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD is a wire-level construct. Implementations SHOULD NOT store ATTR_DISCARD markers in the Adj-RIB-In or Loc-RIB (an implementation MAY retain them temporarily for diagnostic purposes).
When forwarding a route whose wire bytes contain ATTR_DISCARD markers, the forwarding policy () determines whether the marker is included in the outbound UPDATE.

section.name: Route Selection
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD MUST NOT affect BGP best path selection or be used as a tiebreaker.

section.name: Route Filtering
section.t: Operators MAY configure route filters that match on the presence of ATTR_DISCARD markers. This allows policies such as:

ul.li: Rejecting routes where an upstream speaker detected a malformation (preferring routes with clean attribute sets).
Accepting routes with ATTR_DISCARD but logging an alert for operator review.

Filtering based on the (code, reason) pairs, for example rejecting routes where ORIGIN or AS_PATH was discarded, as RFC 7606 specifies treat-as-withdraw (not attribute discard) for these attributes and such a marker indicates a non-conforming upstream implementation. Alternatively, filtering only on malformation-based discards (reason codes 2-3) while ignoring EBGP invalid discards (reason code 1).

section.t: Note: An ATTR_DISCARD marker whose (code, reason) pair references a well-known mandatory attribute — such as ORIGIN (1), AS_PATH (2), or NEXT_HOP (3) — with a malformation-based reason code (2 or 3) indicates a non-conforming upstream implementation. specifies treat-as-withdraw, not attribute discard, for these attributes. Operators SHOULD treat such markers as evidence of upstream misconfiguration or software defects.
Such filtering is a local policy decision and not mandated by this specification.

section.name: Interaction with BMP
section.t: Implementations that export UPDATE messages via the BGP Monitoring Protocol (BMP) will include ATTR_DISCARD markers in the exported Route Monitoring messages, since BMP preserves the wire format of received UPDATEs. Route monitoring stations and analytics tools consuming BMP feeds SHOULD recognize the ATTR_DISCARD attribute code to provide accurate diagnostics rather than reporting it as an unknown attribute.

section.name: Interaction with Extended Messages
section.t: RFC 8654 allows BGP messages up to 65535 bytes when the Extended Message capability has been negotiated. The ATTR_DISCARD mechanism operates on individual attribute headers within the path attributes section and is independent of the overall message size. No special handling is required for extended messages; the overwrite procedure is identical.

section.name: Interaction with BGP Role
section.t: RFC 9234 defines the BGP Role capability and the Only To Customer (OTC) attribute for route leak prevention. OTC validation operates independently of ATTR_DISCARD: OTC violations require route rejection (treat-as-withdraw per RFC 9234 Section 6), not attribute discard. The two mechanisms do not conflict. If an UPDATE triggers both a malformed attribute (requiring attribute discard) and an OTC violation (requiring treat-as- withdraw), the strongest action applies per RFC 7606 Section 3.h, resulting in treat-as-withdraw for the entire UPDATE.

section.name: Interaction with ADD-PATH
section.t: RFC 7911 adds a Path Identifier to NLRI entries. ATTR_DISCARD operates on path attributes, not NLRI, and has no interaction with ADD-PATH.

section.name: Interaction with Graceful Restart
section.t: ATTR_DISCARD has no effect on Graceful Restart processing. ATTR_DISCARD markers do not affect the End-of-RIB marker or stale route handling.

section.name: Interaction with Route Refresh
section.t: When a BGP speaker re-advertises routes in response to a Route Refresh request , ATTR_DISCARD markers are subject to the forwarding policy () like any other outbound UPDATE.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: When generated by in-place overwrite, the value bytes beyond the (code, reason) pair are zeroed by the generating speaker (, step 7). When generated by rebuild (), all value bytes contain (code, reason) pairs and there are no trailing zeroed bytes. In both cases, no remnant of the original malformed data is propagated.

However, an ATTR_DISCARD marker received from a peer may not have been generated by a conforming implementation of this specification. Non-conforming or earlier implementations might leave the original value bytes in place. When an in-place-overwritten marker is received, implementations SHOULD only parse the first (code, reason) pair and SHOULD NOT assume trailing bytes are zeroed.
An attacker who can inject BGP UPDATE messages could include ATTR_DISCARD attributes:

ul.li: Non-transitive injections are silently dropped by the first conforming speaker, limiting impact.

Transitive injections propagate like any unrecognized optional transitive attribute. This is no worse than injecting any other unrecognized optional transitive attribute, which is already possible without this specification.

section.t: Reason code 4 (Local Policy) indicates deliberate attribute removal. Receivers MUST NOT treat reason code 4 as evidence that the removal was operationally justified or authorized. Any BGP speaker can claim reason code 4; the reason code provides diagnostic context, not trust. Operators SHOULD treat all received ATTR_DISCARD markers as advisory.

Implementations that compute cryptographic signatures over UPDATE messages (e.g., BGPsec ) MUST apply ATTR_DISCARD AFTER signature verification but BEFORE any re-signing or forwarding. Modifying attribute bytes before verification would invalidate the signature. Note that applying ATTR_DISCARD to a BGPsec- signed UPDATE also invalidates the signature for downstream verification, requiring the speaker to re-sign the UPDATE. In BGPsec deployments, the zero-copy benefit of ATTR_DISCARD is limited to local processing; outbound forwarding requires re-signing regardless. The re-signed UPDATE will include the ATTR_DISCARD marker as authenticated content; downstream BGPsec receivers will see the marker as a signed attribute and SHOULD parse the (code, reason) pairs normally.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests IANA to allocate a code point from the "BGP Path Attributes" registry:

table.name: IANA BGP Path Attributes Registry Request

section.t: This allocation is requested under the "Standards Action" policy per the existing BGP Path Attributes registry. Any unassigned value is acceptable.

Unlike most BGP path attributes, ATTR_DISCARD does not have a fixed value for the Transitive bit. The Transitive bit is inherited from the original overwritten attribute (). The IANA registry entry MUST note that the attribute is "Optional; Transitive bit varies" to prevent implementations from applying RFC 7606 Section 3.c Attribute Flags validation against a fixed expected value. A non-transitive ATTR_DISCARD is valid (it indicates the original attribute was non-transitive), and MUST NOT be rejected as a flags error.

Note: Value 255 is marked "Reserved for development" per RFC 2042 and is not requested by this document. Implementations that use 255 for experimental purposes would conflict with a permanent assignment.
This document also requests IANA to create a new registry titled "ATTR_DISCARD Reason Codes" with the following initial entries:
The registration policy for values 5-254 is "IETF Review" . Value 0 is the default when no specific reason applies and MUST NOT be reassigned.

section.name: Why Not Strip and Rebuild?
section.t: The straightforward implementation of attribute-discard is to rebuild the path attributes section (shifting bytes, updating lengths, invalidating caches).

The ATTR_DISCARD approach overwrites the attribute in place: the header bytes (flags, type code) plus the entire value (first byte set to the original type code, second byte set to the reason code, remaining bytes zeroed). The write cost is proportional to the attribute's value length, but this is bounded by the individual attribute size (typically a few bytes to a few hundred bytes), not the total path attributes section. No shifting, no cache invalidation. Since attribute- discard is an error-handling path (not every UPDATE), the cost of zeroing is negligible compared to the cost of rebuilding the buffer.

Note that the zero-copy benefit applies primarily to the receive path and intra-AS forwarding. The "strip" and "propagate" forwarding policies () may require modifying flags or rebuilding path attributes on the outbound path. BGPsec deployments require re-signing regardless ().

section.name: Why Not Leave the Original Bytes?
section.t: An alternative is to leave the malformed bytes in place and add the attribute code to a per-UPDATE "discard set" that is checked by every consumer.
RFC 7606 states that "The attribute MUST be discarded." An attribute whose bytes remain in the wire buffer has not been discarded.
The approach also has practical drawbacks:

ul.li: Every code path that reads path attributes must check the discard set. Missing a check silently re-introduces the malformed data.
The discard set must be threaded through all function calls that access the path attributes -- a pervasive API change.
Forwarding code that copies wire bytes directly would inadvertently forward the malformed attribute unless it also checks the discard set.

section.t: The ATTR_DISCARD approach modifies the wire bytes once at the point of detection. The malformed attribute is replaced by a well-defined marker: the flags, type code, and first two value bytes are overwritten, so the malformed attribute ceases to exist in the wire buffer.

section.name: Why a New Attribute Code?
section.t: One could argue that overwriting with any unrecognized code (e.g., 0 or a random value) achieves the same effect. However:

ul.li: A standardized code distinguishes "attribute was discarded per RFC 7606" from "unknown attribute received from peer."
Counters and logging can track ATTR_DISCARD occurrences separately.
The value carries (code, reason) pairs. This is only meaningful with a standardized type code; a random code would not signal that the value has defined semantics.

section.name: Why Inherit Transitivity?
section.t: An earlier version of this design forced ATTR_DISCARD to be always non-transitive, ensuring the marker never propagated. Inheriting the original attribute's Transitive bit was chosen instead for several reasons:

ul.li: It preserves the original attribute's propagation semantics. A transitive attribute was intended to reach all speakers along the path; replacing it with a non-transitive marker silently changes the UPDATE's propagation characteristics.
Transitive markers are visible along the AS path. Non-transitive markers remain local.
The forwarding policy () allows operators to strip, inherit, or propagate markers as needed.

It simplifies the overwrite operation. When the Transitive bit is inherited, only the Optional bit needs to be set, the type code overwritten, and the first two value bytes written -- the flags byte may not need to change at all if the original attribute was already optional and transitive.

section.name: Why Overwrite the First Two Value Bytes?
section.t: The first two value bytes of the ATTR_DISCARD attribute are overwritten with the original attribute's type code and a reason code. This adds two bytes of write overhead (4 bytes total instead of 2 for the header):

ul.li: Receivers can identify which attributes were discarded and why from the (code, reason) pairs without out-of-band information.
The reason code enables automated classification of discard events into EBGP invalid, error-based (invalid length, malformed value), and local policy categories.

The 2-byte minimum requirement is safe for the vast majority of attribute-discard cases, which involve attributes with correct lengths of 4 or more bytes. For the rare cases where the value length is less than 2 (zero-length or single-byte malformed attributes), the implementation uses conventional removal and inserts an ATTR_DISCARD attribute (, step 5).

section.t: The remaining value bytes (bytes 2 through N-1) are zeroed (, step 7). This ensures that no remnant of the malformed attribute's data persists in the wire buffer, eliminating information leakage concerns (). The write cost is proportional to the attribute's value length, but since attribute- discard is an error-handling path rather than normal UPDATE processing, this cost is negligible. No data movement or byte shifting is required regardless of the zeroing cost.

section.name: Acknowledgments
section.t: The author thanks the members of the IDR working group for their review and feedback.
